verses.flow: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God, and Timothy the brother, to the assembly of God that is in Corinth, together with all the holy ones who are in the whole of Achaia:
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, G652) denotes one sent with delegated authority, not merely a messenger. "Ekklēsia" (ἐκκλησία, G1577) literally "called-out assembly," drawn from LXX usage for the congregation of Israel. HISTORICAL: Achaia was the Roman senatorial province comprising southern Greece, with Corinth as its capital — Paul addresses not only the Corinthian congregation but all believers in the broader region.

verses.flow: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort,
verses.note: GREEK: "Eulogētos" (εὐλογητός, G2128) is a verbal adjective meaning "blessed, worthy of praise," used in the NT exclusively of God (cf. Rom 1:25; Eph 1:3). "Oiktirmōn" (οἰκτιρμῶν, G3628) denotes deep compassion or bowels of mercy. "Paraklēsis" (παράκλησις, G3874) carries a dual sense of comfort and exhortation — the same root as "Paraklētos" used of the Holy Spirit (John 14:16). TYPOLOGY: The berakah formula "Blessed be the God and Father" echoes Jewish liturgical blessing patterns (cf. Gen 9:26; 1 Kgs 1:48; Ps 72:18), now reframed through the revelation that God is specifically the Father of the Lord Jesus Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: the one comforting us in all our affliction, so that we are able to comfort those in every affliction, through the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God.
For just as the sufferings of Christ abound unto us, so also through Christ our comfort abounds.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Pathēmata tou Christou" (τὰ παθήματα τοῦ Χριστοῦ) — the sufferings of Christ that overflow unto believers are not redemptive in the atoning sense but participatory; the apostle shares in Christ's afflictions as a member of His body (cf. Phil 3:10; Col 1:24). The parallel structure — sufferings abound, comfort abounds — reveals a divine economy where suffering and consolation are proportionally linked through union with Christ.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: And whether we are afflicted, it is for your comfort and salvation; or whether we are comforted, it is for your comfort, which is made effective in the patient endurance of the same sufferings which we also suffer.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And our hope concerning you is firm, knowing that as you are sharers of the sufferings, so also of the comfort.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: For we do not wish you to be ignorant, brothers, concerning our affliction that came about in Asia, that we were burdened exceedingly, beyond our power, so that we despaired even of living.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kath' hyperbolēn hyper dynamin" (καθ' ὑπερβολὴν ὑπὲρ δύναμιν) is an intensified expression: "according to excess, beyond power." "Exaporēthēnai" (ἐξαπορηθῆναι, G1820) is a strengthened form of "aporeomai" — to be utterly at a loss, completely without resource. HISTORICAL: The affliction in Asia likely refers to events in or around Ephesus, possibly the riot described in Acts 19:23-41, or an otherwise unrecorded mortal threat during Paul's extended Ephesian ministry.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: But we ourselves have had the sentence of death in ourselves, so that we should not be trusting in ourselves but in God who raises the dead,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Apókrima tou thanátou" (ἀπόκριμα τοῦ θανάτου) — "the sentence of death" is a rare term (hapax in NT, G610), drawn from legal language meaning an official judicial verdict. Paul experienced an inward sentencing to death, a divine pedagogy designed to redirect trust from self to "the God who raises the dead" — a title echoing the second benediction of the Shemoneh Esreh and affirming bodily resurrection as foundational to apostolic hope.

verses.flow: who delivered us out of so great a death, and will deliver; in whom we have set our hope that He will also yet deliver us,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: you also laboring together on our behalf by prayer, so that from many faces the gift granted to us through many may be given thanks for on our behalf.
verses.note: GREEK: "Synypourgoúntōn" (συνυπουργούντων, G4943) is a compound hapax legomenon: syn- (together) + hypo- (under) + ergon (work), literally "laboring together under" — depicting the Corinthians as co-laborers in intercession. "Prosōpon" (πρόσωπον, G4383) here means "face/person," yielding the image of many faces turned upward in thanksgiving for the grace given through many prayers.

verses.flow: For our boasting is this: the testimony of our conscience, that in holiness and sincerity of God, not in fleshly wisdom but in the grace of God, we conducted ourselves in the world, and more abundantly toward you.
For we write no other things to you than what you read or also acknowledge, and I hope that you will acknowledge unto the end,
just as you also acknowledged us in part, that we are your boast even as you also are ours, in the day of our Lord Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The day of our Lord Jesus" (ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τοῦ κυρίου Ἰησοῦ) refers to the eschatological day of Christ's return and judgment (cf. 1 Cor 1:8; Phil 1:6, 10). TYPOLOGY: This echoes the OT "Day of the Lord" (yom YHWH) motif (Isa 2:12; Joel 2:1; Amos 5:18), now christologically identified — the day of YHWH is the day of Jesus, affirming His divine identity and sovereign role in final judgment.

verses.flow: And in this confidence I was intending to come to you first, so that you might have a second grace,
and to pass through you into Macedonia, and again from Macedonia to come to you, and by you to be sent on my way into Judea.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore intending this, did I then act with fickleness? Or the things I purpose, do I purpose according to the flesh, so that with me there should be the Yes, yes and the No, no?
But God is faithful, that our word to you is not Yes and No.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For the Son of God, Jesus Christ, the one having been proclaimed among you through us — through me and Silvanus and Timothy — did not become Yes and No, but in Him it has become Yes.
For as many as are the promises of God, in Him is the Yes; therefore also through Him is the Amen, to God for glory through us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: All the promises of God — however many they are (ὅσαι) — find their "Yes" (Ναί) in Christ. He is the fulfillment and confirmation of every divine covenant promise. The "Amen" (ἀμήν) spoken "through Him" indicates that the worshiping community ratifies God's faithfulness through Christ as mediator. TYPOLOGY: This verse functions as a hermeneutical key: every OT promise, whether Abrahamic, Davidic, or new-covenantal, converges and is confirmed in the person of Jesus Christ (cf. Luke 24:44; Rom 15:8-9).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now the one establishing us together with you into Christ, and having anointed us, is God,
verses.note: GREEK: "Chrisas" (χρίσας, G5548) from "chriō" — to anoint — is the verbal root of "Christos" (Anointed One). Paul creates deliberate wordplay: God establishes believers "into Christ" (eis Christon) and "anointed" (chrisas) them. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Anointing language connects believers to the royal-priestly anointing tradition of Israel's kings, priests, and prophets, now extended to all who are incorporated into the Anointed One (cf. 1 John 2:20, 27).

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: who also sealed us and gave the pledge of the Spirit in our hearts.
verses.note: GREEK: "Sphragisámenos" (σφραγισάμενος, G4972) — having sealed, denoting ownership, authentication, and protection. "Arrabōna" (ἀρραβῶνα, G728) is a Semitic loanword (Hebrew 'eravon, cf. Gen 38:17-18) meaning a down payment or pledge guaranteeing full future payment. THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit is both the seal of divine ownership and the earnest — the first installment — of the coming inheritance, guaranteeing the fullness of eschatological redemption (cf. Eph 1:13-14; 4:30).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But I call upon God as witness against my soul, that it was to spare you that I came no more to Corinth.
Not that we lord it over your faith, but we are fellow workers of your joy, for by faith you stand.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kyrieúomen" (κυριεύομεν, G2961) — "we lord it over," from kyrios (lord). Paul deliberately uses dominion language to deny authoritarian control over their faith. "Synergoi" (συνεργοί, G4904) — fellow workers, co-laborers. THEOLOGICAL: Apostolic authority is not despotic sovereignty over believers' faith but collaborative service toward their joy. The closing affirmation "by faith you stand" (tē pistei hestēkate) anchors the believer's stability in faith rather than in human leadership (cf. Rom 11:20; 1 Cor 16:13).

verses.flow: For I determined this for myself: not again in grief to come to you.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For if I grieve you, then who is the one making me glad except the one being grieved by me?

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I wrote this very thing, so that having come I might not have grief from those from whom I ought to rejoice, having confidence in you all that my joy is the joy of you all.
For out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote to you through many tears, not that you might be grieved, but that you might know the love which I have more abundantly toward you.
verses.note: GREEK: "thlipsis" (θλῖψις, Strong's G2347) denotes pressing affliction or tribulation, and "synochē" (συνοχή, G4928) means a pressing together or anguish — both terms convey intense inner distress. Paul distinguishes his motive: not to cause grief (lypē) but to make known his abundant love (agapē). The phrase "through many tears" (dia pollōn dakryōn) reveals the deeply personal cost of apostolic correction.

verses.flow: But if anyone has caused grief, he has caused grief not to me, but in part — that I may not be too severe — to you all.
Sufficient to such a one is this punishment, the one by the majority.
verses.note: GREEK: "Epitimia" (ἐπιτιμία, G2009) is a NT hapax legomenon, appearing only here in the entire New Testament. It denotes a penalty or censure formally imposed, likely by the congregation acting together. The phrase "by the majority" (hypo tōn pleionōn) implies a formal communal disciplinary action, reflecting early church governance practices.

verses.flow: So that on the contrary, you should rather forgive and comfort him, lest by excessive grief such a one be swallowed up.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's pastoral counsel moves from discipline to restoration, reflecting the heart of God who desires repentance rather than destruction (Ezek 18:23). The verb "katapothē" (καταποθῇ, swallowed up) is strong — the same word used in 1 Cor 15:54 for death being "swallowed up" in victory. Excessive grief without restoration serves Satan's purposes rather than God's.

verses.flow: Therefore I urge you to confirm your love toward him.
For to this end also I wrote, that I might know the proof of you, whether you are obedient in all things.
But to whom you forgive anything, I also forgive; for indeed what I have forgiven, if I have forgiven anything, it is for your sakes in the presence of Christ,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "In the presence of Christ" (en prosōpō Christou) indicates that all forgiveness within the community takes place before the face of the risen Lord, who is the ultimate judge and mediator. Paul models apostolic authority exercised not autonomously but in union with Christ and for the sake of the community (cf. Matt 18:20).

verses.flow: so that we might not be taken advantage of by Satan, for we are not ignorant of his schemes.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Satan" (Σατανᾶς, G4567) here functions as the adversary who exploits divisions within God's assembly. The term "noēmata" (νοήματα, G3540) means schemes, devices, or thought-patterns — indicating Satan's strategic intelligence against the people of God. Paul implies that unforgiveness becomes a foothold by which the adversary gains ground within the covenant community (cf. Eph 4:27).

verb perfect passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: Now having come to Troas for the gospel of Christ, and a door having been opened to me in the Lord,

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I had no rest in my spirit, because I did not find Titus my brother; but having taken leave of them, I went out into Macedonia.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: But thanks be to God, the One always leading us in triumphal procession in Christ, and manifesting through us the fragrance of the knowledge of Him in every place.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Thriambeuonti" (θριαμβεύοντι, G2358) refers to the Roman triumphal procession in which a victorious general paraded through the streets with captives and the burning of incense. Paul recasts this imagery: God is the conquering general, believers are led in His train, and the knowledge of Christ spreads like incense along the route. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the imagery of God as divine warrior-king leading His people (Ps 68:18; cf. Eph 4:8).

words.m: verb present passive participle dative plural masculine
verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we are a sweet aroma of Christ to God, among those being saved and among those perishing —
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Euōdia" (εὐωδία, G2175) — sweet aroma — deliberately echoes the Levitical sacrificial language of the LXX, where offerings produce a "fragrance of a sweet smell" pleasing to God (Gen 8:21; Lev 1:9). Believers in Christ become living sacrificial offerings whose very existence is an aroma ascending to God, fulfilling the priestly vocation of Israel (Exod 19:6; 1 Pet 2:5, 9).

verses.flow: to the ones indeed an odor from death unto death, but to the others an odor from life unto life. And who is sufficient for these things?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The same gospel message produces opposite effects: life unto life for those being saved, and death unto death for those perishing. This parallels Isaiah 6:9-10, where the prophetic word simultaneously reveals and hardens. Paul's rhetorical question "who is sufficient?" (tis hikanos) anticipates his answer in 3:5 — sufficiency comes from God alone.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we are not as the many, peddling the word of God, but as from sincerity, but as from God, before God, in Christ we speak.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kapēleuontes" (καπηλεύοντες, G2585) is another rare NT term meaning to peddle, huckster, or trade in for profit — originally used of wine-dealers who diluted their product. Paul contrasts such mercenary handling of God's word with speaking from sincerity (eilikrineia, G1505, lit. "judged by sunlight") and from God Himself. HISTORICAL: Itinerant philosophers and sophists in the Greco-Roman world were frequently accused of teaching for profit, and Paul distances his ministry from such practices.

verses.flow: Do we begin again to commend ourselves? Or do we not need, as some do, letters of commendation to you, or from you?

verses.flow: Our letter is you yourselves, having been written upon our hearts, being known and being read by all men,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: being made manifest that you are a letter of Christ, having been ministered by us, having been written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of hearts of flesh.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul draws a direct contrast with the Sinai covenant. The "tablets of stone" (plaxin lithinais) echo Exodus 31:18 and 34:1 (LXX), where God inscribed the Torah on stone tablets. Now the living God inscribes His law upon human hearts through the Spirit, fulfilling the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:33 (LXX Jer 38:33) and Ezekiel 36:26. GREEK: The term sarkinos (σάρκινος, "of flesh") denotes material composition rather than moral weakness—these are hearts made of living flesh rather than dead stone.

verses.flow: And such confidence we have through Christ toward God—
not that we are sufficient of ourselves to reckon anything as from ourselves, but our sufficiency is from God,
who also made us sufficient as ministers of a new covenant, not of the letter but of the Spirit; for the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes the fundamental distinction between the old and new covenants. The "letter" (gramma, γράμμα) refers not to Scripture itself but to the written code apart from the Spirit's life-giving power. Paul's phrase "ministers of a new covenant" (diakonous kainēs diathēkēs) directly echoes Jesus' words at the Last Supper (Luke 22:20) and the prophecy of Jeremiah 31:31. GREEK: The verb zōopoiei (ζῳοποιεῖ, "gives life," Strong's G2227) is the same word used of resurrection power in 1 Corinthians 15:22, 45—the Spirit's ministry is nothing less than the bestowal of eschatological life.

verses.flow: But if the ministry of death, having been engraved in letters upon stones, came into being in glory, so that the sons of Israel were not able to gaze upon the face of Moses on account of the glory of his face—the glory which was being brought to nothing—
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul references Exodus 34:29-35, where Moses' face shone with the reflected glory (doxa, δόξα) of the Lord after receiving the covenant at Sinai. The participle katargoumenēn ("being brought to nothing") indicates that this glory was by nature transitory and provisional, designed to give way to a greater glory. HISTORICAL: The "ministry of death" is not a dismissal of Torah but a recognition that the law, apart from the Spirit, could only expose sin and pronounce its sentence (Rom 7:10).

verses.flow: how shall not rather the ministry of the Spirit be in glory?
For if the ministry of condemnation had glory, much more does the ministry of righteousness abound in glory.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present active participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: For indeed that which had been glorified has not been glorified in this respect, on account of the surpassing glory.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: For if that which is being brought to nothing came through glory, much more that which remains is in glory.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Having therefore such a hope, we use much boldness,

words.m: verb present passive participle genitive singular neuter

verses.flow: and not as Moses, who placed a veil upon his face, so that the sons of Israel might not gaze upon the end of that which was being brought to nothing.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul offers a striking reinterpretation of Exodus 34:33-35. In the Exodus narrative, Moses veiled his face after speaking to the people. Paul reveals a deeper purpose: Moses veiled his face so that Israel would not perceive the fading (telos, τέλος—"end" or "termination") of the old covenant glory. GREEK: The term telos here means "cessation" or "outcome" rather than "goal," emphasizing the temporary design of the Mosaic economy.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: But their minds were hardened. For until this present day the same veil remains upon the reading of the old covenant, not being lifted, because in Christ it is being brought to nothing.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verb epōrōthē (ἐπωρώθη, "were hardened," Strong's G4456) denotes a calcifying or petrifying—a deep spiritual obduracy. Paul transfers the veil from Moses' face to Israel's perception: it now covers their understanding when the old covenant is read. The phrase "in Christ it is being brought to nothing" (en Christō katargeitai) reveals that Christ is the one who removes the veil and fulfills the old covenant's provisional purpose. TYPOLOGY: The "old covenant" (palaias diathēkēs) is the earliest occurrence of this exact phrase in the NT, establishing the two-covenant framework.

verses.flow: But until today, whenever Moses is read, a veil lies upon their heart.
But whenever one turns to the Lord, the veil is taken away.
Now the Lord is the Spirit, and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most theologically dense verses in the Pauline corpus. "The Lord is the Spirit" (ho kyrios to pneuma estin) identifies the risen Christ with the operative power of the Spirit without collapsing their persons—rather, it means that the "Lord" to whom one turns in v.16 (echoing Exod 34:34, LXX) is now encountered in and through the Spirit. GREEK: Eleutheria (ἐλευθερία, Strong's G1657) denotes not mere license but the freedom from the veil, from the condemnation of the letter, and from the hardening described in vv.14-15. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The divine glory (doxa) that once radiated from the Lord's presence on Sinai now transforms believers through the Spirit—the mediatorial veil is removed because Christ is the ultimate divine mediator.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative singular neuter
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But we all, with unveiled face beholding as in a mirror the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image, from glory to glory, even as from the Lord, the Spirit.
verses.note: GREEK: The verb katoptrizomenoi (κατοπτριζόμενοι, a NT hapax legomenon, Strong's G2734) can mean either "beholding as in a mirror" or "reflecting as a mirror." Both senses may be intended: believers both perceive and radiate the Lord's glory. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "from glory to glory" (apo doxēs eis doxan) describes progressive, eschatological transformation. The term metamorphoumetha (μεταμορφούμεθα, "are being transformed") is the same verb used of Christ's transfiguration (Matt 17:2; Mark 9:2). TYPOLOGY: Where Moses' glory faded, believers' glory increases. The "image" (eikōn) into which they are transformed is the image of Christ, the true Eikon of God (2 Cor 4:4; Col 1:15), reversing the defacement of the divine image in Adam (Gen 1:26-27).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: On account of this, having this ministry, even as we received mercy, we do not lose heart,
verses.note: GREEK: "Diakonían" (διακονίαν, Strong's G1248) denotes a ministry of service, here referring back to the new covenant ministry described in chapter 3. "Ēleḗthēmen" (ἠλεήθημεν) is aorist passive—"we were shown mercy"—emphasizing that the ministry itself is a gift of divine mercy, not human achievement. "Enkakоûmen" (ἐγκακοῦμεν, G1573) means to grow weary or lose heart, a term Paul returns to in v.16 forming an inclusio around the chapter.

verses.flow: but we have renounced the hidden things of shame, not walking in craftiness nor handling deceitfully the word of God, but by the manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every conscience of men before God.

verses.flow: But even if our gospel is veiled, among those who are perishing it is veiled,
in whom the god of this age has blinded the minds of the unbelieving, so that the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God, should not shine upon them.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Ho theòs toû aiōnos toútou" (ὁ θεὸς τοῦ αἰῶνος τούτου)—"the god of this age"—refers to Satan as a subordinate spiritual being exercising delegated and usurped authority over the present evil age. This reflects the divine council worldview in which lesser elohim/spiritual powers operate under God's sovereign permission (cf. Job 1:6-12; Eph 2:2; John 12:31). THEOLOGICAL: "Eikṓn toû theoû" (εἰκὼν τοῦ θεοῦ)—Christ as "the image of God"—is a profound Christological declaration linking Jesus to the visible manifestation of the invisible God (Col 1:15), and echoing the imago Dei of Gen 1:26-27, of which Christ is the perfect and ultimate fulfillment.

verses.flow: For we do not proclaim ourselves, but Christ Jesus as Lord, and ourselves as your bondservants on account of Jesus.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For God, the One having said, "Out of darkness light shall shine," is He who shone in our hearts for the illumination of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Christ.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul's quotation echoes Gen 1:3 (LXX) where God commanded light into existence at creation. The new creation in Christ is thus typologically patterned on the original creation—God's creative word bringing light from darkness. GREEK: "Phōtismón" (φωτισμόν, G5462) means "illumination" or "enlightenment," used only here and in v.4 in the NT. "En prosṓpōi Christoû" (ἐν προσώπῳ Χριστοῦ)—"in the face of Christ"—connects back to the unveiled face motif of 3:18, where Moses' veiled glory gives way to Christ's unveiled and surpassing glory.

verses.flow: But we have this treasure in earthen vessels, so that the surpassing greatness of the power may be of God and not from ourselves—
verses.note: GREEK: "Ostrakínois skeúesin" (ὀστρακίνοις σκεύεσιν)—"earthen vessels" or "clay jars"—refers to cheap, fragile pottery, emphasizing the contrast between the transcendent treasure of the gospel and the frail humanity of its bearers. THEOLOGICAL: "Hē hyperbolḗ tēs dynámeōs" (ἡ ὑπερβολὴ τῆς δυνάμεως)—"the surpassing greatness of the power"—the deliberate fragility of the vessel ensures that the extraordinary power is attributed to God alone and not to human instrumentality (cf. Judg 7:2; 1 Cor 1:27-29).

verses.flow: being persecuted, but not forsaken; being cast down, but not destroyed;

verses.flow: always carrying about in the body the dying of Jesus, so that also the life of Jesus may be manifested in our body.
verses.note: GREEK: "Nékrōsin" (νέκρωσιν, G3500) denotes the "putting to death" or "dying process," not death itself (thánatos). It is the ongoing experience of mortal suffering that mirrors Christ's passion. TYPOLOGY: Paul sees apostolic suffering as a continual re-enactment of the death-and-resurrection pattern of Jesus—the dying of Jesus borne in the body becomes the occasion for the resurrection life of Jesus to be made visible. This anticipates the fuller treatment of union with Christ's death and life in Rom 6:3-11.

verses.flow: For always we who live are being handed over to death on account of Jesus, so that also the life of Jesus may be manifested in our mortal flesh.
So then, death is at work in us, but life in you.

verses.flow: But having the same spirit of faith according to what has been written, "I believed, therefore I spoke," we also believe, therefore also we speak,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Psalm 116:10 (LXX Ps 115:1)—"I believed, therefore I spoke"—applying the psalmist's trust amid mortal danger to his own apostolic experience. The "same spirit of faith" (tò autò pneûma tēs písteōs) links the faith of the old covenant saints with that of new covenant believers, demonstrating continuity across the covenants. GREEK: "Pneûma" (πνεῦμα) here likely carries a double sense: both the disposition or attitude of faith, and the Holy Spirit who produces that faith.

verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: knowing that the One who raised the Lord Jesus will raise us also with Jesus and will present us together with you.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: For all things are for your sake, so that the grace, having abounded through the many, may cause thanksgiving to overflow to the glory of God.
Therefore we do not lose heart, but even if our outer man is being destroyed, yet our inner man is being renewed day by day.
For the momentary lightness of our affliction is working for us an eternal weight of glory beyond all surpassing measure,
verses.note: GREEK: "Parautíka" (παραυτίκα, G3910) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "momentary" or "just for the present." Paul pairs it with "elaphrón" (ἐλαφρόν, "light/slight") to create a deliberate contrast with "aiṓnion báros dóxēs" (αἰώνιον βάρος δόξης)—"eternal weight of glory." THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "kath' hyperbolḗn eis hyperbolḗn" (καθ' ὑπερβολὴν εἰς ὑπερβολήν) means literally "according to surpassing unto surpassing"—an intensifying idiom expressing immeasurable excess. The Hebrew concept of "glory" (kābôd) itself carries the root meaning of "heaviness/weight," making "weight of glory" a profound wordplay connecting Greek and Hebrew theology.

verses.flow: we not looking at the things being seen, but at the things not being seen; for the things being seen are temporary, but the things not being seen are eternal.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between "tà blepómena" (τὰ βλεπόμενα, "the things being seen") and "tà mḗ blepómena" (τὰ μὴ βλεπόμενα, "the things not being seen") establishes the eschatological framework governing the entire chapter. Present afflictions belong to the visible, temporal order; the glory being prepared belongs to the unseen, eternal order. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as the theological capstone of chapter 4, grounding the paradoxes of vv.8-12 and the hope of vv.14-17 in an inaugurated eschatology where faith apprehends what sight cannot yet perceive (cf. Heb 11:1).

verses.flow: For we know that if our earthly house of the tent be dissolved, we have a building from God, a house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens.
verses.note: GREEK: "Skēnos" (σκῆνος, Strong's G4636) means "tent" or "tabernacle," a rare word used as a metaphor for the mortal body. "Acheiropoíētos" (ἀχειροποίητος, G886) means "not made with hands," a compound word emphasizing divine rather than human construction. TYPOLOGY: The tabernacle/tent imagery recalls Israel's wilderness tabernacle (Exod 26), a temporary dwelling anticipating the permanent temple—here Paul applies the same pattern to the believer's body awaiting its heavenly, glorified form (Phil 3:21).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For indeed in this we groan, longing to be clothed upon with our dwelling which is from heaven,

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: if indeed, having been clothed, we shall not be found naked.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For indeed we who are in the tent groan, being burdened, in that we do not wish to be unclothed, but to be clothed upon, so that the mortal might be swallowed up by life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's desire is not disembodiment ("unclothed") but transformation ("clothed upon"), reflecting the biblical hope not of escape from the body but of bodily resurrection in which mortality is absorbed by life. The language of "swallowed up" (katapínō) echoes Isaiah 25:8 LXX and 1 Cor 15:54, where death itself is swallowed in victory.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now the One having worked us for this very thing is God, the One having given to us the earnest of the Spirit.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katergasámenos" (κατεργασάμενος, G2716) means "having worked" or "having prepared," indicating God's purposeful, effectual labor. "Arrabṓn" (ἀρραβών, G728) is a Semitic loanword meaning "earnest" or "down payment"—a commercial term denoting a guarantee of full future payment. THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit is not merely a gift but a pledge guaranteeing the fullness of resurrection life to come (Eph 1:13-14).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Being confident therefore always, and knowing that while being at home in the body we are absent from the Lord—
for through faith we walk, not through sight—
yet we are confident and are well-pleased rather to be absent from the body and to be at home with the Lord.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore also we make it our aim, whether being at home or being absent, to be well-pleasing to Him.
For we must all be made manifest before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive the things done through the body, according to what he practiced, whether good or worthless.
verses.note: GREEK: "Bēma" (βῆμα, G968) refers to the raised platform where a Roman magistrate rendered judgment; Paul may have stood before such a seat in Corinth before Gallio (Acts 18:12). THEOLOGICAL: This is not a judgment of salvation but of deeds done in the body—each believer will give account before Christ as righteous Judge. "Phaûlon" (φαῦλον, G5337) means "worthless" or "base," not merely morally evil but that which is of no account.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Knowing therefore the fear of the Lord, we persuade men; but to God we have been made manifest, and I hope also to have been made manifest in your consciences.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: We are not commending ourselves again to you, but are giving you occasion of boasting on our behalf, so that you may have an answer toward those boasting in appearance and not in heart.
For if we were beside ourselves, it was for God; if we are of sound mind, it is for you.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For the love of Christ constrains us, having judged this: that One died on behalf of all, therefore all died;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The love of Christ constrains us" (synéchei, G4912)—the verb means to hold together, press from every side, or compel. Paul's logic is substitutionary: if One died "on behalf of" (hypér) all, then in that representative death all died. This is the ground of both justification and the ethical imperative of verse 15. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the representative who dies for the many echoes the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53:4-6, 11-12.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: and He died on behalf of all, so that those who live might no longer live for themselves, but for the One having died and been raised on their behalf.
So then, from now on we know no one according to the flesh; even if we have known Christ according to the flesh, yet now we know Him thus no longer.
So then, if anyone is in Christ—new creation! The old things have passed away; behold, all things have become new.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ktísis" (κτίσις, G2937) means "creation" or "creature"—Paul uses the term without a verb, yielding the exclamatory: "new creation!" This echoes the LXX language of Isaiah 43:18-19 and 65:17 where God declares He is making all things new. THEOLOGICAL: The "new creation" is both individual transformation and participation in the eschatological renewal inaugurated by Christ's resurrection. The old age has passed; the new age of the Messiah has broken in.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And all things are from God, the One having reconciled us to Himself through Christ, and having given to us the ministry of reconciliation,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: namely, that God was in Christ reconciling the world to Himself, not reckoning to them their trespasses, and having placed in us the word of reconciliation.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "God was in Christ reconciling the world to Himself" is one of the most concentrated Christological statements in Paul's letters, affirming both the divine initiative in salvation and God's presence and action in and through the person of Christ. "Not reckoning" (mē logizómenos) uses the same accounting term Paul employs in Romans 4:8 (Ps 32:2 LXX), linking reconciliation to the non-imputation of trespasses.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: On behalf of Christ therefore we serve as ambassadors, as though God were entreating through us; we implore on behalf of Christ: be reconciled to God!

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: The One having known no sin, He made to be sin on our behalf, so that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the heart of Pauline atonement theology. "The One having known no sin"—Christ's sinlessness is the necessary ground for His substitutionary work. "He made to be sin" (hamartían epoíēsen)—God treated the sinless Christ as the bearer of sin itself, likely evoking the sin offering (ḥaṭṭā't) of Leviticus 4-5 where the same Hebrew word means both "sin" and "sin offering." TYPOLOGY: Christ fulfills the Day of Atonement typology (Lev 16), bearing the people's sin so that believers might become "the righteousness of God" in Him—a great exchange of status (Isa 53:4-6; Rom 8:3-4).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And working together, we also beseech you not to receive the grace of God in vain—
verses.note: GREEK: Synergountes (συνεργοῦντες, Strong's G4903) is a present active participle meaning "working together with," implying co-laboring with God Himself. The verb déchomai (δέξασθαι) in the aorist infinitive suggests a decisive act of receiving grace—Paul warns against receiving it eis kenon (εἰς κενόν), "unto emptiness," i.e., to no effect.

verses.flow: for He says, "In an acceptable time I heard you, and in a day of salvation I helped you." Behold, now is the well-acceptable time; behold, now is the day of salvation—
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Isaiah 49:8 (LXX), originally addressed to the Servant of the Lord concerning Israel's restoration. Paul applies the prophetic kairos (καιρός, "appointed season") to the present age of gospel proclamation, declaring that the eschatological "day of salvation" has broken into the present through Christ. GREEK: Euprosdektos (εὐπρόσδεκτος, G2144) intensifies the LXX's dektos—not merely "acceptable" but "very favorably received."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: giving no cause of stumbling in anything, so that the ministry might not be blamed,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: but in everything commending ourselves as servants of God: in much endurance, in afflictions, in necessities, in distresses,
verses.note: GREEK: Synistánontes (συνιστάνοντες, G4921) means "commending" or "demonstrating"—Paul presents credentials not of letters but of suffering. Diakonoi (διάκονοι) here denotes "servants/ministers" of God, not the later technical office. The catalogue of hardships that follows (vv. 4-10) forms a peristasis catalogue, a Greco-Roman rhetorical device Paul transforms to demonstrate apostolic legitimacy through suffering rather than worldly honor.

verses.flow: in stripes, in imprisonments, in tumults, in labors, in watchings, in fastings,
in pureness, in knowledge, in longsuffering, in kindness, in the Holy Spirit, in love unhypocritical,
in the word of truth, in the power of God, through the weapons of righteousness on the right hand and on the left,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "weapons of righteousness on the right and on the left" (hóplon tēs dikaiosynēs) encompasses both offensive weapons (right hand, the sword hand) and defensive ones (left hand, the shield hand). This martial imagery anticipates the fuller armor-of-God passage in Ephesians 6:13-17, grounding the apostolic ministry in spiritual warfare waged through divine power (dynamei theou).

verses.flow: through glory and dishonor, through evil report and good report; as deceivers and yet true,

verses.flow: as unknown and yet well-known, as dying and behold we live, as being chastened and yet not put to death,
