verses.flow: as sorrowful yet always rejoicing, as poor yet making many rich, as having nothing and yet possessing all things.
Our mouth has been opened to you, Corinthians; our heart has been made wide.
You are not restricted in us, but you are restricted in your own affections.
Now in the same recompense—I speak as to children—be widened also yourselves.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Do not become unequally yoked with unbelievers. For what partnership has righteousness with lawlessness? Or what fellowship has light with darkness?
verses.note: GREEK: Heterozygountes (ἑτεροζυγοῦντες, G2086) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "yoked with a different kind," drawn from the LXX background of Deuteronomy 22:10 (prohibiting yoking ox and donkey together) and Leviticus 19:19. TYPOLOGY: The four rhetorical questions (vv. 14-16a) set up irreconcilable antitheses—righteousness/lawlessness (anomia), light/darkness—echoing the dualistic categories of Second Temple Judaism and the prophetic tradition of covenant separation.

verses.flow: And what accord has Christ with Beliar? Or what part has a believer with an unbeliever?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Beliar (Βελιάρ) is a variant of Belial, a name for Satan prevalent in Second Temple literature (Jubilees, Testament of the Twelve Patriarchs, and the Dead Sea Scrolls' War Scroll). Paul sets Christ and Beliar as opposing cosmic rulers, reflecting divine council theology where the Anointed One stands supreme against the chief adversary. This is the only NT occurrence of this name.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And what agreement has the temple of God with idols? For we are the temple of the living God, even as God said, "I will dwell in them and walk among them, and I will be their God, and they shall be My people."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul weaves together Leviticus 26:11-12 and Ezekiel 37:27, both covenant-indwelling promises originally given to Israel, now applied to the church as the naos (ναός, "inner sanctuary/temple") of the living God. The shift from physical temple to the believing community marks the eschatological fulfillment of God's dwelling-presence. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The declaration "I will be their God" (esomai autōn theos) is the covenant formula par excellence, asserting YHWH's unique claim over His people against all rival elohim/idols.

verses.flow: Therefore, "Come out from the midst of them and be separated," says the Lord, "and touch no unclean thing, and I will receive you,"
"and I will be to you a Father, and you shall be to Me sons and daughters," says the Lord Almighty.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This composite quotation draws from 2 Samuel 7:14 (the Davidic covenant promise, "I will be to him a Father") and Isaiah 43:6, expanded to include "daughters"—a remarkable Pauline extension of the Davidic sonship to all believers, male and female. THEOLOGICAL: The title pantokratōr (παντοκράτωρ, G3841), "Almighty" or "Ruler of All," appears frequently in the LXX for YHWH Sabaoth ("Lord of Hosts") and in Revelation, but only here in Paul's letters, underscoring the sovereign divine authority behind these covenant promises.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore having these promises, beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of flesh and spirit, bringing holiness to completion in the fear of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Defilement" (molysmós, G3436) is a NT hapax legomenon, found only here. It denotes staining or pollution, drawn from the imagery of ritual contamination. "Bringing to completion" (epiteloúntes, from epitéleō) implies an ongoing process of perfecting holiness, not a one-time act. THEOLOGICAL: The dual cleansing of "flesh and spirit" encompasses the totality of human existence — both outward conduct and inward disposition — to be purified before God. The "promises" (epangelíai) refer back to the catena of OT quotations in 2 Cor 6:16-18, including God's dwelling with His people (Lev 26:12; Ezek 37:27).

verses.flow: Make room for us. We wronged no one, we corrupted no one, we took advantage of no one.
I do not speak for condemnation, for I have said before that you are in our hearts, to die together and to live together.
Great is my boldness toward you, great is my boasting on your behalf. I have been filled with the comfort; I am overflowing with joy in all our affliction.
verses.note: GREEK: "Overflowing" (hyperperisseúomai, G5248) is an intensified compound — hyper + perisseúō — expressing joy beyond all measure. "Boldness" (parrhēsía, G3954) denotes freedom of speech and open confidence, a term with political resonance in Greek democratic discourse, here applied to Paul's apostolic relationship with the Corinthians.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For indeed, when we came into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest, but we were afflicted in every way — fightings without, fears within.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Macedonia was the Roman province in northern Greece where Philippi and Thessalonica were located. Paul traveled there from Troas (2 Cor 2:12-13) hoping to find Titus with news from Corinth. The "fightings without, fears within" (éxōthen máchai, ésōthen phóboi) likely refers to external opposition from both Jewish and pagan adversaries and internal anxiety over the Corinthian church's response to his severe letter.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But God, the one who comforts the lowly, comforted us by the coming of Titus,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul identifies God with the participle "the one who comforts the lowly" (ho parakalōn toùs tapeinoús), echoing the LXX of Isaiah 49:13 and Psalm 137:6 (LXX). This is a divine title — God's character is defined by His consolation of the humbled. The parousia of Titus becomes the instrument of divine comfort, showing God's providence working through human agents.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and not only by his coming, but also by the comfort with which he was comforted over you, reporting to us your longing, your mourning, your zeal on my behalf, so that I rejoiced even more.
For even if I grieved you by the letter, I do not regret it — even if I did regret it — for I see that that letter, even if only for an hour, grieved you.
Now I rejoice, not that you were grieved, but that you were grieved unto repentance; for you were grieved according to God, so that in nothing you might suffer loss from us.
verses.note: GREEK: "Repentance" (metánoia, G3341) means a change of mind and heart, not merely remorse. "According to God" (katà theón) indicates grief aligned with God's own perspective on sin — sorrow that leads to transformation. THEOLOGICAL: Paul carefully distinguishes between sorrow that is merely emotional pain and sorrow that is oriented toward God and therefore produces genuine repentance. The purpose clause "so that in nothing you might suffer loss from us" (hína en mēdení zēmiōthēte) shows Paul's pastoral intent was restorative, not punitive.

verses.flow: For the grief that is according to God works repentance unto salvation, not to be regretted; but the grief of the world works death.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse contains one of the most important NT distinctions on the nature of repentance. "Grief according to God" (katà theón lýpē) produces repentance leading to salvation — and this is ametamélēton (G278, "not to be regretted," or "irrevocable"). By contrast, "the grief of the world" (hē toû kósmou lýpē) — mere worldly remorse without orientation toward God — produces death. TYPOLOGY: The contrast echoes the difference between Peter's weeping unto restoration (Luke 22:62) and Judas's remorse unto destruction (Matt 27:3-5).

verses.flow: For behold, this very thing — the being grieved according to God — what earnestness it produced in you, but also what defense, but also what indignation, but also what fear, but also what longing, but also what zeal, but also what vindication! In everything you demonstrated yourselves to be pure in the matter.
verses.note: GREEK: Paul employs a powerful rhetorical device with sevenfold repetition of allá ("but also"), creating an escalating list: spoudḗ (earnestness), apología (defense), aganáktēsis (indignation), phóbos (fear), epipóthēsis (longing), zēlos (zeal), ekdíkēsis (vindication/punishment). STRUCTURE: The sevenfold list likely signifies completeness — their repentance was thorough in every dimension. "Pure" (hagnoús) in this matter demonstrates their corporate innocence once the discipline was carried out.

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: So then, even if I wrote to you, it was not on account of the one who did wrong, nor on account of the one who was wronged, but on account of your earnestness on our behalf being made manifest to you before God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul reveals the deeper purpose of his severe letter: not primarily punitive justice for offender or offended, but the manifestation (phaneróō) of the Corinthians' own earnestness "before God" (enṓpion toû theoû). The divine audience is paramount — their response demonstrated their loyalty not merely to Paul but in the sight of God Himself.

verses.flow: On account of this we have been comforted. And upon our comfort, we rejoiced still more abundantly at the joy of Titus, because his spirit has been refreshed by all of you.
For if I have boasted anything to him on your behalf, I was not put to shame; but as we spoke all things to you in truth, so also our boasting before Titus proved to be truth.

words.m: verb present passive participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And his affections are more abundantly toward you, as he remembers the obedience of you all, how with fear and trembling you received him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Affections" (splánchna, G4698) literally means "intestines" or "bowels," understood in antiquity as the seat of deep emotions and compassion. "Fear and trembling" (phóbou kaì trómou) is a Pauline phrase (cf. Phil 2:12; 1 Cor 2:3; Eph 6:5) denoting reverent seriousness, not servile terror. Their reception of Titus reflected their regard for apostolic authority.

verses.flow: I rejoice that in everything I have confidence in you.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: Now we make known to you, brothers, the grace of God that has been given in the churches of Macedonia,
verses.note: GREEK: "Gnōrizomen" (γνωρίζομεν, Strong's G1107) means "we make known, we certify." "Charis" (χάρις, G5485) here denotes God's grace as the operative cause behind the Macedonians' generosity. Paul frames the entire collection theology under grace, not obligation. HISTORICAL: The churches of Macedonia include Philippi, Thessalonica, and Berea—congregations founded during Paul's second missionary journey (Acts 16-17).

verses.flow: that in a great testing of affliction, the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their generosity,
verses.note: GREEK: "Dokimē thlipseōs" (δοκιμῇ θλίψεως) means "testing of affliction"—dokimē (G1382) denotes proven character through trial. "Haplotēs" (ἁπλότης, G572) denotes single-hearted generosity without ulterior motive. The paradox is deliberate: deep poverty (kata bathous ptōcheia) overflowed into rich generosity.

verses.flow: that according to their ability, I bear witness, and beyond their ability, of their own accord,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: with much entreaty begging of us the grace and the fellowship of the ministry unto the saints—
and not as we had hoped, but they gave themselves first to the Lord and to us through the will of God,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sequence is critical—"they gave themselves first to the Lord and then to us." Self-surrender to Christ precedes and grounds all acts of service. Paul grounds the collection not in social obligation but in covenant devotion to the Lord (kyrios), establishing Christ's lordship as the foundation of Christian generosity.

verses.flow: so that we urged Titus that, just as he had previously begun, so also he would complete among you this grace also.
But just as you abound in everything—in faith and in word and in knowledge and in all earnestness and in the love from us that is in you—that you abound also in this grace.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I speak not by way of command, but through the earnestness of others, testing also the genuineness of your love.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that being rich, on account of you he became poor, that you through his poverty might become rich.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most compressed Christological statements in Paul. "Plousios ōn" (being rich) refers to Christ's pre-incarnate divine glory (cf. Phil 2:6; John 17:5). "Eptōcheusen" (ἐπτώχευσεν, G4433) is an ingressive aorist—he entered into a state of poverty through the incarnation. The purpose clause (hina) reveals substitutionary exchange: his poverty becomes the means of our enrichment. TYPOLOGY: This echoes the divine condescension pattern found throughout Scripture—God stooping to lift up the lowly (1 Sam 2:7-8; Ps 113:5-8).

verses.flow: And I give a judgment in this matter, for this is profitable for you, who began beforehand not only the doing but also the willing from a year ago.
But now complete also the doing, so that just as there was the readiness of the willing, so also there may be the completing out of what you have.
For if the readiness is present, it is acceptable according to whatever one has, not according to what one does not have.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Prothymia" (προθυμία, G4288), readiness or eagerness, is the criterion of acceptable giving before God. Paul establishes a principle of proportionality: God evaluates the gift according to what one possesses (katho ean echē), not according to what one lacks. This echoes Jesus' teaching on the widow's mite (Mark 12:41-44).

verses.flow: For this is not that there be relief for others and affliction for you, but out of equality—
in the present time your abundance for their lack, that also their abundance may come to be for your lack, so that there may be equality,
as it has been written: "The one who gathered much did not have excess, and the one who gathered little did not have lack."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Exodus 16:18 (LXX) regarding the gathering of manna. The manna principle—divine equalization of provision—becomes a type for the mutual sharing among the body of Christ. Just as God ensured no Israelite had surplus or deficit in the wilderness, so the new covenant community is to reflect this equality through voluntary generosity. GREEK: "Epleonasen" (ἐπλεόνασεν, G4121) means "had excess/surplus"; "ēlattonēsen" (ἠλαττόνησεν, G1641) means "had less/lacked."

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: But thanks be to God, the one giving the same earnestness on your behalf in the heart of Titus,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: for he indeed received the exhortation, but being more earnest, of his own accord he went out to you.
And we sent together with him the brother whose praise in the gospel is through all the churches—

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: and not only so, but who was also appointed by the churches as our traveling companion with this grace that is being ministered by us, unto the glory of the Lord himself and our readiness—
verses.note: GREEK: "Cheirotonētheis" (χειροτονηθείς, G5500) literally means "having been stretched out the hand," referring to election by show of hands—an early church democratic practice for appointing delegates. "Synekdēmos" (συνέκδημος, G4898) is a rare compound meaning "traveling companion," used only here and in Acts 19:29. HISTORICAL: This unnamed brother was formally appointed by the churches as an accountability measure for the collection funds, reflecting early church governance wisdom.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: taking precaution in this, lest anyone should blame us in this abundance that is being ministered by us.
For we take forethought for what is honorable, not only before the Lord, but also before men.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul alludes to Proverbs 3:4 (LXX): "Take forethought for what is honorable before the Lord and before men." The principle of financial transparency and public accountability in ministry is grounded in Scripture. "Pronoumen" (προνοοῦμεν, G4306) means to give careful thought beforehand—Paul insists on integrity not only in God's sight (enōpion kyriou) but also before human observers.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: And we sent together with them our brother, whom we have tested in many things many times, being earnest, but now much more earnest by great confidence toward you.
Whether concerning Titus—he is my partner and fellow worker unto you—or our brothers—they are apostles of the churches, a glory of Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostoloi ekklēsiōn" (ἀπόστολοι ἐκκλησιῶν) means "apostles of the churches"—here apostolos (G652) is used in its broader sense of "sent ones, delegates, emissaries" rather than the technical sense of the Twelve. "Doxa Christou" (δόξα Χριστοῦ, "glory of Christ") is a striking predicate nominative: these brothers, in their very persons and mission, reflect and manifest the glory of Christ.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore show to them the proof of your love and of our boasting on your behalf, before the face of the churches.
verses.note: GREEK: "Endeiknymenoi" (ἐνδεικνύμενοι, G1731) is a middle participle functioning as an imperative—"show forth, demonstrate." "Eis prosōpon tōn ekklēsiōn" (εἰς πρόσωπον τῶν ἐκκλησιῶν) means "unto the face of the churches," indicating that the Corinthians' response will be visible testimony before the broader Christian community. Paul closes the appeal by connecting love, boasting, and public witness.

verses.flow: For concerning the ministry that is unto the saints, it is superfluous for me to write to you,
for I know your readiness, of which I boast on your behalf to the Macedonians, that Achaia has been prepared since last year, and your zeal has stirred up the greater number.
verses.note: GREEK: "Readiness" (προθυμίαν, prothymian, Strong's G4288) denotes eagerness or willingness of mind — a forward disposition. "Zeal" (ζῆλος, zēlos, G2205) can mean fervor or jealous emulation; here it carries the positive sense of ardent enthusiasm. Paul's boasting to the Macedonians about Achaia's preparedness created a holy provocation to generosity among the churches.

words.m: verb perfect middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But I sent the brothers, so that our boasting on your behalf might not be made empty in this matter, so that, even as I was saying, you might be prepared,
lest somehow, if Macedonians come with me and find you unprepared, we — not to say you — should be put to shame in this confidence.

words.m: verb perfect middle participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: Therefore I considered it necessary to exhort the brothers that they should go before unto you and arrange in advance your previously promised blessing, that this might be ready thus as a blessing and not as covetousness.
verses.note: GREEK: "Arrange in advance" (προκαταρτίσωσι, prokatartisōsi, G4294) is a rare compound verb — only here in the NT — meaning to prepare or complete beforehand. "Previously promised" (προεπηγγελμένην, proepēngelmenēn, G4279) is likewise a NT hapax legomenon, emphasizing that this blessing had been pledged earlier. THEOLOGICAL: Paul carefully distinguishes blessing (εὐλογίαν) from covetousness (πλεονεξίαν), framing generous giving as an act of worship rather than compulsion or greed.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And this: the one sowing sparingly, sparingly also shall reap; and the one sowing upon blessings, upon blessings also shall reap.
Each one as he has purposed in the heart — not out of grief, nor out of compulsion — for God loves a cheerful giver.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul's quotation draws on Proverbs 22:8 (LXX), where God blesses the cheerful and generous giver. "Purposed" (προῄρηται, proērētai, G4255) is in the perfect middle, indicating a deliberate, settled decision of the heart. GREEK: "Cheerful" (ἱλαρόν, hilaron, G2431) is the root of English "hilarious" — it denotes not mere willingness but joyful delight. "Giver" (δότην, dotēn, G1395) appears only here in the NT.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And God is able to make all grace abound unto you, so that in everything, at all times, having all sufficiency, you may abound unto every good work,
as it has been written: "He scattered abroad, he gave to the poor; his righteousness abides unto the age."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct quotation of Psalm 112:9 (LXX Psalm 111:9), a psalm celebrating the righteous man who fears the Lord. Paul applies it to the generous believer whose acts of mercy constitute an enduring righteousness. The "scattering abroad" (ἐσκόρπισεν) paradoxically produces increase, foreshadowing the gospel principle that self-giving results in abundance (cf. John 12:24).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the one who supplies seed to the sower and bread for food shall supply and multiply your seed, and shall increase the fruits of your righteousness,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul weaves together Isaiah 55:10 and Hosea 10:12 (LXX), presenting God as the sovereign source of both physical provision ("seed to the sower and bread for food") and spiritual fruitfulness ("fruits of your righteousness"). The agricultural metaphor ties material generosity to the harvest of divine righteousness, showing that God multiplies what is given sacrificially.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: being enriched in everything unto all generosity, which works through us thanksgiving to God —

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: because the ministry of this service is not only supplying the lacks of the saints, but also abounding through many thanksgivings to God —
verses.note: GREEK: "Ministry" (διακονία, diakonia, G1248) and "service" (λειτουργίας, leitourgias, G3009) are paired deliberately. Leitourgia originally denoted public service or a civic duty, but in the LXX it became a technical term for priestly temple service. THEOLOGICAL: Paul thus elevates the collection for the Jerusalem saints to the status of a liturgical, priestly act — material giving becomes an offering before God that simultaneously meets human need and generates corporate worship.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: through the proving of this ministry, glorifying God upon the submission of your confession unto the gospel of Christ, and upon the generosity of your fellowship unto them and unto all,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: and by their supplication on your behalf, longing for you on account of the surpassing grace of God upon you.
Thanks be to God for his indescribable gift!
verses.note: GREEK: "Indescribable" (ἀνεκδιηγήτῳ, anekdiēgētō, G411) is a NT hapax legomenon — literally "unable to be fully narrated." The word's intensive alpha-privative construction emphasizes that God's gift transcends all human expression. THEOLOGICAL: While the immediate context is generosity, this doxological exclamation points beyond the collection to God's ultimate gift in Christ (cf. Rom 8:32; John 3:16), the gift that makes all human generosity both possible and meaningful.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now I myself, Paul, exhort you through the meekness and gentleness of Christ — I who face to face indeed am lowly among you, but being absent am bold toward you —
verses.note: GREEK: "Praÿtēs" (πραΰτης, Strong's G4240) denotes meekness or gentle strength, not weakness; "epieíkeia" (ἐπιείκεια, G1932) means yielding clemency or forbearance. Paul appeals through the very character of Christ Himself. The ironic contrast between "lowly face to face" and "bold when absent" reflects accusations from opponents at Corinth.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: But I beseech you, that being present I may not need to be bold with the confidence with which I reckon to be daring against some who reckon us as walking according to the flesh.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For though walking in the flesh, we do not wage war according to the flesh —

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: for the weapons of our warfare are not fleshly, but powerful before God for the tearing down of strongholds — casting down reasonings,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul employs military metaphor — "hopla" (ὅπλα, G3696) are instruments of warfare, and "ochyrōmata" (ὀχυρώματα, G3794) are fortified strongholds. The weapons are "dynata tō theō" — powerful before God or mighty through God. This frames the apostolic mission as spiritual warfare against entrenched ideological and spiritual opposition, not mere human conflict (cf. Eph 6:12-17).

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular neuter
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and every high thing raising itself up against the knowledge of God, and taking captive every thought into the obedience of Christ,
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Hypsōma" (ὕψωμα, G5313) — "high thing" or "exalted thing" — may carry cosmic overtones, as in Rom 8:39 where it appears alongside angelic powers. Every elevated thing that sets itself against "the knowledge of God" (tēs gnōseōs tou theou) is to be brought low. "Aichmalōtízontes" (taking captive) every thought into obedience to Christ asserts Christ's sovereign lordship over the noetic realm itself.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: and having in readiness to avenge all disobedience, whenever your obedience should be fulfilled.
You look at things according to appearance. If anyone has persuaded himself to be of Christ, let him reckon this again from himself, that even as he is of Christ, so also are we.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ta kata prosōpon blepete" can be read as indicative ("You look at what is before your face") or imperative ("Look at what is before your face"). The context favors the indicative as a rebuke. Paul challenges the Corinthians' superficial assessment and asserts that his own belonging to Christ is no less than that of any rival claimant.

verses.flow: For even if I should boast somewhat more abundantly concerning our authority, which the Lord gave for building up and not for tearing down of you, I shall not be put to shame,
that I may not seem as though I would terrify you through the letters.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For "His letters," he says, "are weighty and strong, but the presence of the body is weak, and the word contemptible."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Let such a one reckon this, that what we are in word through letters while absent, such also are we in deed while present.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we do not dare to classify or compare ourselves with some of those commending themselves; but they, measuring themselves by themselves and comparing themselves with themselves, do not understand.
But we will not boast beyond measure, but according to the measure of the rule which God apportioned to us as a measure — to reach even as far as you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kanōn" (κανών, G2583) originally meant a measuring reed or rule, hence a defined sphere or standard. Paul uses it to denote the divinely apportioned territory of his apostolic mission. "Ametra" (ἄμετρα, G280) — "beyond measure" or "without measure" — is a rare term emphasizing that Paul refuses to claim credit outside his God-given assignment.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we do not overextend ourselves as though not reaching to you, for we came even as far as you in the gospel of Christ —

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: not boasting beyond measure in the labors of others, but having hope that, as your faith is growing, we shall be enlarged among you according to our rule abundantly,
so as to proclaim the gospel in the regions beyond you, not to boast in another's rule concerning things made ready.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct citation of Jer 9:24 (LXX 9:23): "Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord." Paul applies this prophetic principle — that all legitimate glory must be referred back to God — as the governing ethic for apostolic ministry. The same text is quoted in 1 Cor 1:31, forming an inclusio across the Corinthian correspondence.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For it is not the one commending himself who is approved, but the one whom the Lord commends.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Dokimos" (δόκιμος, G1384) means tested and approved, as of metals refined by fire. The ultimate criterion of legitimacy is not self-commendation but divine commendation. Paul thus undercuts the self-promoting "super-apostles" (cf. 2 Cor 11:5) by grounding all authentic ministry in the Lord's own verdict rather than human evaluation.

verses.flow: Would that you bore with me in a little of folly—but indeed, you do bear with me.
For I am jealous over you with a jealousy of God, for I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "ζηλῶ" (zēloō, G2206) denotes a fierce, protective jealousy. "ἡρμοσάμην" (harmozomai, G718) is a middle voice meaning "I betrothed," used of the father or friend who arranges a marriage. Paul presents himself as the spiritual father who betrothed the Corinthian assembly to Christ as a pure bride. TYPOLOGY: The imagery of Israel as YHWH's bride (Hos 2:19-20; Isa 54:5) is here applied to Christ and the church, equating Christ with the divine Bridegroom of the Old Testament (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7-9).

verses.flow: But I fear lest somehow, as the serpent deceived Eve by his craftiness, your thoughts should be corrupted from the simplicity and the purity that is toward Christ.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul reaches back to Genesis 3:1-13, identifying the serpent's deception of Eve as the archetype of spiritual seduction. The Corinthians face the same primal threat—corruption from "simplicity" (ἁπλότης, haplotēs, G572, meaning singleness or sincerity) toward Christ. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The serpent figure here is linked to the cosmic adversary who works through false teachers, echoing the spiritual warfare framework of the divine council where rebellious heavenly beings oppose God's purposes.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For if indeed the one coming proclaims another Jesus whom we did not proclaim, or you receive a different spirit which you did not receive, or a different gospel which you did not accept, you bear with it well enough.
For I reckon myself to have come behind in nothing compared to the super-apostles.
verses.note: GREEK: "ὑπερλίαν ἀποστόλων" (hyperlian apostolōn)—"super-apostles" is likely Paul's ironic term. "ὑπερλίαν" (G5244) is a compound adverb meaning "exceedingly beyond," used with biting sarcasm. Most scholars distinguish these from the Twelve; Paul refers to the rival teachers at Corinth who claimed superior apostolic authority.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But even if I am unskilled in speech, yet not in knowledge; but in every way, having made this manifest in all things toward you.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Or did I commit a sin by humbling myself that you might be exalted, because freely I proclaimed to you the gospel of God?

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Other assemblies I robbed, taking wages for the ministry unto you.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And being present with you and having been in want, I was a burden to no one; for the brothers who came from Macedonia supplied my lack. And in everything I kept myself without burden to you, and I shall keep myself so.
The truth of Christ is in me, that this boasting shall not be stopped against me in the regions of Achaia.
Why? Because I do not love you? God knows.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But what I do, I also shall do, that I may cut off the occasion from those desiring an occasion, that in that which they boast they may be found even as we.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For such ones are false apostles, deceitful workers, disguising themselves as apostles of Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: "ψευδαπόστολοι" (pseudapostoloi, G5570) is a hapax legomenon in the NT—occurring only here. "μετασχηματιζόμενοι" (metaschēmatizomenoi, G3345) means "disguising" or "transforming the outward appearance," implying a deliberate external masquerade without inward change. THEOLOGICAL: Paul unmasks his opponents not merely as mistaken but as agents of deception operating under satanic influence (vv.14-15).

verses.flow: And no wonder, for Satan himself disguises himself as an angel of light.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan's disguise as an "angel of light" (ἄγγελον φωτός) reflects the Second Temple Jewish tradition that rebellious heavenly beings can present themselves in deceptive glory. This is foundational for understanding spiritual discernment: the adversary operates not primarily through obvious evil but through counterfeit luminosity. THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes a critical hermeneutical principle—false teaching often appears outwardly glorious and spiritual (Gal 1:8).

verses.flow: It is no great thing, therefore, if also his ministers disguise themselves as ministers of righteousness, whose end shall be according to their works.
Again I say, let no one think me to be foolish—but if otherwise, even as foolish receive me, that I also might boast a little.
What I speak, I speak not according to the Lord, but as in folly, in this confidence of boasting.
Since many boast according to the flesh, I also shall boast.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For gladly you bear with fools, being wise yourselves.
For you bear with it if someone enslaves you, if someone devours you, if someone takes from you, if someone exalts himself, if someone strikes you on the face.
I speak to my dishonor, as though we had been weak. But in whatever anyone is bold—I speak in folly—I also am bold.
Are they Hebrews? I also. Are they Israelites? I also. Are they seed of Abraham? I also.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Paul establishes three layers of Jewish identity: "Hebrews" (Ἑβραῖοι)—those retaining Hebrew/Aramaic language and culture; "Israelites" (Ἰσραηλῖται)—those belonging to the covenant people by descent; "seed of Abraham" (σπέρμα Ἀβραάμ)—heirs of the Abrahamic promise. These distinctions mattered greatly in Second Temple Judaism where Diaspora and Palestinian Jews held varying degrees of cultural prestige. TYPOLOGY: Paul's claim to Abrahamic seed anticipates his argument that true Abrahamic inheritance is fulfilled in Christ (Gal 3:16, 29).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Are they ministers of Christ?—I speak as one beside himself—I am more so: in labors more abundantly, in imprisonments more abundantly, in beatings beyond measure, in deaths often.
verses.note: GREEK: "παραφρονῶν" (paraphronōn, G3912) means "speaking as one out of his mind"—another hapax legomenon. Paul's catalogue of sufferings (peristasis catalogue) was a recognized rhetorical form in Greco-Roman literature, but Paul inverts the convention: where philosophers boasted of endurance proving virtue, Paul boasts of weakness proving Christ's power. HISTORICAL: The catalogue that follows (vv.23-27) provides biographical data about Paul not found in Acts, indicating additional imprisonments, beatings, and shipwrecks beyond what Luke recorded.

verses.flow: From the Jews five times I received forty stripes less one.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "forty stripes less one" refers to the synagogue punishment described in Deuteronomy 25:3. Jewish law prescribed a maximum of forty lashes; the practice of stopping at thirty-nine was a safeguard against exceeding the limit. That Paul received this five times indicates he remained under synagogue jurisdiction and did not sever himself from the Jewish community, even as they disciplined him for his proclamation of Christ.

verses.flow: Three times I was beaten with rods, once I was stoned, three times I was shipwrecked, a night and a day I have spent in the deep.

In journeys often, in perils of rivers, in perils of robbers, in perils from my own nation, in perils from the Gentiles, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness, in perils in the sea, in perils among false brothers,
in labor and toil, in sleeplessness often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness.
Apart from the things without, there is the daily pressure upon me—the anxiety for all the assemblies.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐπίστασις" (epistasis, G1999) can mean either "pressure/burden" or "attention/care"—the daily weight of oversight. "μέριμνα" (merimna, G3308) is the same word Jesus used for worldly anxiety (Matt 6:25), but here Paul applies it to pastoral care. THEOLOGICAL: After the external catalogue of physical sufferings, Paul names the internal burden as the climax—the daily anxiety for all the assemblies reveals that apostolic suffering is fundamentally relational and pastoral, not merely physical.

verses.flow: Who is weak, and I am not weak? Who is caused to stumble, and I do not burn?
If it is necessary to boast, I shall boast in the things of my weakness.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: The God and Father of the Lord Jesus, he who is blessed unto the ages, knows that I do not lie.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul invokes a solemn oath formula—"The God and Father of the Lord Jesus, he who is blessed unto the ages, knows that I do not lie." The doxological insertion ("blessed unto the ages") elevates this beyond mere assertion to covenant testimony before God. This oath bridges the catalogue of sufferings (vv.23-28) and the Damascus escape narrative (vv.32-33), grounding all of Paul's claims in divine witness. GREEK: "εὐλογητός" (eulogētos, G2128) is reserved in the NT almost exclusively for God (Rom 1:25; 9:5; Eph 1:3).

verses.flow: In Damascus the ethnarch of Aretas the king was guarding the city of the Damascenes in order to seize me,
and through a window in a basket I was let down through the wall, and I escaped his hands.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: To boast is necessary, though it is not profitable; but I will come to visions and revelations of the Lord.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: I know a man in Christ, fourteen years ago—whether in the body I do not know, or outside the body I do not know, God knows—such a one was caught up to the third heaven.
verses.note: GREEK: "Caught up" (harpagenta, ἁρπαγέντα, Strong's G726) is an aorist passive participle of harpazō, meaning "seized" or "snatched away" by divine force. "Third heaven" (trítou ouranoú) reflects Jewish cosmology in which the highest heaven is the dwelling of God Himself (cf. 1 Kgs 8:27; Testament of Levi 2-3). Paul speaks of himself in the third person, a convention of apocalyptic humility.

verses.flow: And I know such a man—whether in the body or apart from the body I do not know, God knows—

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: that he was caught up into Paradise and heard unspeakable words which it is not permitted for a man to speak.
verses.note: GREEK: "Unspeakable words" (árrēta rhḗmata) — árrētos (G731) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "inexpressible" or "not to be spoken." The term was used in Greek mystery religions for sacred secrets, but Paul reappropriates it for genuine divine revelation. THEOLOGICAL: Paradise (parádeisos, G3857) here is equated with the third heaven of v.2, reflecting the Jewish expectation that the eschatological garden of God is located in the highest heavenly realm (cf. Rev 2:7; Luke 23:43).

verses.flow: On behalf of such a one I will boast, but on behalf of myself I will not boast, except in my weaknesses.
For if I should wish to boast, I will not be a fool, for I will speak truth; but I refrain, lest anyone should reckon concerning me beyond what he sees in me or hears from me.

And because of the surpassing greatness of the revelations, therefore, lest I should exalt myself, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, a messenger of Satan, that he might buffet me, lest I should exalt myself.
verses.note: GREEK: "Thorn" (skólops, G4647) literally means "stake" or "sharp pointed object"; its exact referent remains debated. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "messenger of Satan" (ángelos Satanâ) reveals the divine council dynamic—God sovereignly permits a satanic agent to afflict Paul for the purpose of preventing pride, paralleling Job 1-2 where the adversary operates only within divinely granted boundaries. The passive "was given" (edóthē) is a divine passive, indicating God as the ultimate source.

verses.flow: Concerning this, three times I besought the Lord that it might depart from me.

And He has said to me, "My grace is sufficient for you, for power is made complete in weakness." Most gladly therefore I will rather boast in my weaknesses, that the power of Christ may tabernacle upon me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "My grace is sufficient for you" (Arkeî soi hē cháris mou) is the Lord's direct speech to Paul, one of the rare post-ascension dominical sayings. "Is made complete" (teleîtai, G5055) is present passive—power is continually being brought to perfection in weakness. GREEK: "Tabernacle upon" (episkēnōsē, G1981) is a rare verb evoking the Shekinah glory dwelling in the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35). Paul's weakness becomes the tent in which Christ's power takes up residence.

verses.flow: Therefore I take pleasure in weaknesses, in insults, in necessities, in persecutions, and in distresses, for the sake of Christ; for when I am weak, then I am powerful.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox "when I am weak, then I am powerful" (dynatós eimi) encapsulates the cruciform logic of apostolic existence. This inverts all human categories of strength, grounding authentic power solely in dependence upon Christ (cf. 2 Cor 4:7-12; 13:4).

verses.flow: I have become a fool; you compelled me. For I ought to have been commended by you, for in nothing did I fall behind the super-apostles, even if I am nothing.
verses.note: GREEK: "Super-apostles" (hyperlían apostólōn) — hyperlían (G5228 + G3029) is a Pauline coinage meaning "exceedingly great" or "super," used with biting irony for the rival teachers at Corinth who boast in outward credentials (cf. 2 Cor 11:5). HISTORICAL: These figures likely claimed superior visionary experiences and rhetorical skill, fitting a Hellenistic model of the "divine man" (theîos anḗr) that Paul deliberately subverts.

verses.flow: Indeed the signs of the apostle were worked out among you in all endurance, by signs and wonders and mighty works.
verses.note: GREEK: "Signs of the apostle" (sēmeîa toû apostólou) uses the genitive to denote signs that authenticate apostolic office. The triad "signs and wonders and mighty works" (sēmeíois te kaì térasin kaì dynámesin) echoes the LXX language for God's acts in the Exodus (Deut 4:34) and is applied to Jesus in Acts 2:22, placing Paul's ministry in continuity with divine redemptive power.

verses.flow: For what is there in which you were made less than the rest of the churches, except that I myself was not a burden to you? Forgive me this injustice!

Behold, this third time I hold myself ready to come to you, and I will not be a burden; for I seek not the things that are yours but you yourselves. For the children ought not to lay up treasure for the parents, but the parents for the children.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And I most gladly will spend and be utterly spent on behalf of your souls. If I love you more abundantly, am I loved the less?
verses.note: GREEK: "Be utterly spent" (ekdapanēthḗsomai, G1550) is a strengthened compound of dapanáō—the prefix ek- intensifies the verb to mean "expended completely." This is a rare NT word reflecting Paul's total self-expenditure. TYPOLOGY: Paul's willingness to be poured out for his spiritual children echoes Christ's own self-giving love (Phil 2:7-8) and the sacrificial logic of the new covenant ministry (2 Cor 4:10-12).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But let it be so—I myself did not burden you; but being crafty, I took you by guile.
Did I take advantage of you through any of those whom I have sent to you?
I urged Titus, and I sent the brother with him. Did Titus take advantage of you? Did we not walk in the same spirit? Did we not walk in the same steps?
Have you been thinking all along that we are defending ourselves to you? Before God in Christ we speak; and all things, beloved, are for your building up.
verses.note: GREEK: "Before God" (katénanti theoû) emphasizes that Paul's speech is uttered in the very presence of God as witness—a solemn oath formula. "Building up" (oikodomḗs, G3619) is a key Pauline metaphor for the edification of the church as God's temple (cf. 1 Cor 3:9-17; Eph 2:21). THEOLOGICAL: Paul insists that all his apostolic activity—including his "foolish" boasting—serves not self-vindication but the construction of the community of faith.

verses.flow: For I fear lest perhaps, having come, I might find you not such as I wish, and I might be found by you such as you do not wish—lest perhaps there be strife, jealousy, outbursts of anger, rivalries, slanders, whisperings, conceit, disorders;

verses.flow: lest, when I come again, my God should humble me before you, and I should mourn over many of those who have sinned beforehand and have not repented of the uncleanness and sexual immorality and sensuality which they practiced.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul fears encountering unrepentant sin characterized by three terms: "uncleanness" (akatharsía), "sexual immorality" (porneía), and "sensuality" (asélgeia)—a vice triad that appears also in Gal 5:19. HISTORICAL: Corinth's reputation for sexual license, combined with the influence of pagan temple culture, made these sins an acute pastoral concern. The verb "sinned beforehand" (proēmartēkótōn, G4258) indicates these sins predate Paul's previous visit, compounding the gravity of their failure to repent.

verses.flow: This third time I am coming to you. "Upon the mouth of two witnesses and three shall every matter be established."
verses.note: GREEK: "Rhēma" (ῥῆμα, Strong's G4487) here means "matter" or "word" — a legal term for a case or charge. TYPOLOGY: Paul quotes Deuteronomy 19:15 (LXX), applying the Mosaic law of witnesses to his apostolic visits as establishing evidence. His three visits function as three witnesses confirming the charges against the unrepentant.

verses.flow: I have warned beforehand and I warn beforehand, as being present the second time and now being absent, to those who have sinned before and to all the rest, that if I come again, I will not spare,

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: since you seek proof of the Christ speaking in me, who toward you is not weak but is mighty in you.
For indeed He was crucified out of weakness, yet He lives out of the power of God. For indeed we are weak in Him, yet we shall live with Him out of the power of God toward you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents one of the densest summaries of cruciform theology in Paul. Christ's crucifixion "out of weakness" (ἐξ ἀσθενείας) and resurrection life "out of the power of God" (ἐκ δυνάμεως θεοῦ) establishes the paradigm for apostolic ministry — and indeed all Christian existence. GREEK: "Estaurōthē" (ἐσταυρώθη) is aorist passive, pointing to the historical, completed event; "zē" (ζῇ) is present active, indicating His ongoing, present life. The shift from aorist to present is theologically deliberate (cf. Rom 6:9-10).

verses.flow: Examine yourselves whether you are in the faith; prove yourselves. Or do you not recognize yourselves, that Jesus Christ is in you — unless indeed you are unapproved?
verses.note: GREEK: "Peirazete" (πειράζετε, Strong's G3985) means "test" or "examine" — the same word used for testing metals. "Dokimazete" (δοκιμάζετε, Strong's G1381) means "prove by trial" or "approve after testing." "Adokimoi" (ἀδόκιμοι, Strong's G96) is the negative form — "unapproved," "failing the test," not counterfeit metal. THEOLOGICAL: Paul turns the Corinthians' demand for proof of his apostleship (v. 3) back upon them: they should test themselves for evidence of Christ dwelling in them.

verses.flow: But I hope that you will know that we are not unapproved.
Now we pray to God that you do no evil — not that we might appear approved, but that you might do what is good, even though we should be as unapproved.
For we can do nothing against the truth, but only for the truth.
For we rejoice when we are weak and you are strong. And this also we pray for: your restoration to completeness.
verses.note: GREEK: "Katartisin" (κατάρτισιν, Strong's G2676) is a hapax legomenon in the NT. It derives from "katartizō" (to mend, restore, make complete), used in Matt 4:21 for mending nets. The noun denotes a process of being brought to full completeness or proper order. Paul's deepest prayer for Corinth is their full spiritual restoration.

verses.flow: On account of this I write these things while absent, so that being present I might not deal sharply, according to the authority which the Lord gave to me for building up and not for tearing down.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul articulates a principle governing all apostolic authority: it is given by the Lord (kyrios) "for building up" (eis oikodomēn) and "not for tearing down" (ouk eis kathairesin). This echoes Jeremiah 1:10 (LXX) and frames church discipline as ultimately constructive, not destructive. GREEK: "Apotomōs" (ἀποτόμως, Strong's G664) means "sharply" or "severely," occurring only here and in Titus 1:13 in the NT.

verses.flow: Finally, brothers, rejoice; be restored; be exhorted; be of the same mind; be at peace — and the God of love and of peace shall be with you.
Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the saints greet you.
The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit be with you all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is the fullest Trinitarian benediction in the Pauline corpus. The three divine persons are distinguished by distinct operations — grace from the Lord Jesus Christ, love from God (the Father), and fellowship (koinōnia) from the Holy Spirit — yet united in a single blessing. GREEK: "Koinōnia" (κοινωνία, Strong's G2842) denotes participation, partnership, and shared communion — not merely friendly association but ontological sharing in the Spirit's life. This verse became foundational for later creedal Trinitarian formulation (cf. Matt 28:19).
